title: SEGMENT 2 — The three components — antibody, linker, payload

lines.text: SEGMENT 2 — The three components — antibody, linker, payload
Let's break the missile down into its three parts.
If you take nothing else from this segment, take the three-component picture and the reason each component exists.
Picture an antibody — a Y-shape, with two arms reaching up and a stem hanging down.
You've seen this molecule a thousand times.

Its job in nature is to grab a target — a virus, a bacterium, an infected cell — using the variable tips of its arms, while the stem of the Y signals to the rest of the immune system that something needs to be destroyed.
In an ADC, we hijack the grabbing function and add something brand new: we hang a poison off the molecule.
The antibody still grabs, but now the antibody is also carrying a hidden payload, and when the whole package gets pulled inside the target cell, the payload is released and the cell dies.
So component number one is the antibody.
This is the guidance system.

It is typically a fully humanized monoclonal antibody — meaning the protein sequence has been engineered to look as much like a human-made antibody as possible, so the patient's immune system doesn't reject it.

The antibody is chosen for one reason above all others: it has to bind, with high affinity, to a protein that sits on the surface of cancer cells, and ideally a protein that is much more abundant on cancer cells than on healthy cells.
HER2 is the famous example — it's overexpressed on certain breast and gastric cancers at levels twenty to a hundred times higher than on normal tissue.
CD30 sits on Hodgkin lymphoma cells but is essentially absent from healthy tissues.
TROP2 is overexpressed on a wide variety of solid tumors.
CD33 sits on acute myeloid leukemia cells.
Find a surface protein that screams cancer, and you have a target.
But the antibody alone, in most cases, doesn't kill the cancer.
Trastuzumab — the bare antibody against HER2 — works in some patients, but in many others it just sits on the cell surface and the cancer grows anyway.
We need something more lethal hanging off it.
That's where the other two components come in.
Component number two is the payload.
This is the warhead — the cytotoxic drug that does the actual killing.
And here is the crucial point: ADC payloads are not normal chemotherapy drugs.
They are not drugs you could ever give to a patient as a standalone treatment.
They are, almost without exception, two to four orders of magnitude more potent than conventional chemotherapy.
We're talking about molecules that kill cells at picomolar concentrations — that's billionths of millionths of a molar concentration.
If you injected one of these payloads directly into someone's bloodstream at a therapeutic dose, you would almost certainly kill them.
So why do we use such ferociously toxic molecules?
Because of the math.

An antibody can only carry a small number of payload molecules — typically two to eight, sometimes a few more — and once you've targeted that antibody to a tumor cell, only a fraction of the injected dose actually reaches the tumor.
Most ADC drugs deliver only about one percent of their injected dose to the actual tumor cells; the rest gets cleared through normal antibody recycling.
So the payload that does arrive has to be potent enough that those handful of molecules per cell are sufficient to kill the cell.
The whole strategy only works if the warhead is monstrously powerful.
Hold that thought — we'll come back to it when we talk about drug-antibody ratio.
Common payload classes include microtubule poisons — the auristatins like MMAE and MMAF, and the maytansinoids like DM1 and DM4 — which jam up the cell's mitotic spindle and stop division.
There are DNA-damaging agents like the calicheamicins, which create double-strand DNA breaks.
And there are topoisomerase I inhibitors like exatecan and its derivatives, which is what T-DXd carries.
We'll spend a whole segment later on the chemistry of these classes, but for now, just know: the payload is the warhead, and the warhead has to be terrifyingly potent.
Component number three is the linker.
The rope that holds the warhead to the missile.
And this is where the engineering gets subtle and beautiful, because the linker has to do two things that are in tension with each other.
In the bloodstream, the linker has to be stable.
Rock solid.

The whole point of the ADC is to deliver the payload only to the cancer cell — if the linker breaks loose in circulation, the warhead floats free in the patient's blood, and you've just given them naked chemotherapy with all the systemic toxicity you were trying to avoid.
So the linker needs to survive hours to days of circulation, exposure to plasma enzymes, pH changes, you name it.
But once the ADC binds its target on a cancer cell and the whole complex gets pulled inside the cell, the linker has to break.
It has to release the payload.
Inside the right compartment — typically the lysosome, which is the cell's acidic recycling bin — the linker needs to come apart so the warhead is freed to do its job.
If the linker doesn't release, the payload stays stuck to the antibody and doesn't kill anything.
Stable outside, releasable inside.
That's the linker's job.

Think of it as a rope with a controlled-release mechanism — strong in the open air, but designed to dissolve when it hits acid, or get snipped by a specific enzyme, or break under reducing conditions.

There are essentially two big design philosophies for how to do this: cleavable linkers, which break in response to some specific intracellular trigger, and non-cleavable linkers, which require the entire antibody to be digested by the lysosome before the payload is released.
We'll do a whole segment on linker chemistry.
For now: linker stable in blood, releasable in cell.
Three components.
Antibody for targeting.
Payload for killing.
Linker holding them together with controlled release.
That's the architecture.
Every approved ADC, every ADC in clinical trials, every ADC in academic preprints — they're all variations on this same three-part theme.
The art is in the choices.
Which antibody, which payload, which linker, and how do you stick them together.
Let's look at how this art has evolved over twenty-five years of clinical experience, starting with the drug that proved the concept could work — and then proved it could fail spectacularly.
